name: Maternal non-pregnant total plasma cortisol
tier_rationale: Tier A: non-pregnant morning plasma cortisol ~10 ug/dL (wide normal range 5-20). Carr 1981 baseline.

name: Maternal term total plasma cortisol
tier_rationale: Tier B from Carr 1981: total cortisol rises ~3x by term, largely driven by CBG rise; free cortisol also rises modestly.

source_check.quote: As pregnancy advanced, the concentration of cortisol in plasma increased progressively from 149 +/- 34 ng/ml (mean and SEM) at 12 weeks to 352 +/- 90 ng/ml at 26 weeks' gestation but changed minimally thereafter until labor commenced, during which values of 706 +/- 148 ng/ml were achieved.
source_check.extracted_value: Cortisol reached 352 +/- 90 ng/mL (= 35.2 ug/dL) at 26 weeks and 'changed minimally thereafter until labor.' During labor it reached 706 ng/mL (= 70.6 ug/dL). The plateau value 35.2 ug/dL falls within the stored range 25-40.

name: Maternal free T4 at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Glinoer 1997: free T4 falls slightly through gestation; total T4 rises (TBG-mediated).

source_check.source_citation: Hussein et al. 2017, Int J Gen Med (PMC5456279), trimester-specific thyroid reference intervals, Basrah
source_check.quote: For Free T4, the trimester specific reference ranges were 0.8-1.53, 0.7-1.20 and 0.7-1.20 ng/dL for first, second and third trimesters, respectively.
source_check.extracted_value: Third-trimester free T4 5th-95th centile 0.7-1.20 ng/dL with mean 0.90 ng/dL. The reported mean (0.90) equals the stored central (0.9); the stored range 0.7-1.1 closely matches the reported 0.7-1.20.

name: Maternal TSH in first trimester
tier_rationale: Tier B from Glinoer 1997: TSH suppressed in T1 by hCG cross-reactivity at the TSH receptor; recovers by T2/T3.

source_check.source_citation: Hussein et al. 2017, Int J Gen Med (PMC5456279), trimester-specific thyroid reference intervals, Basrah
source_check.quote: Trimester specific normal ranges of TSH in microIU/mL were 0.04-3.77, 0.30-3.21 and 0.6-4.5 microIU/mL, respectively, for first, second and third trimesters.
source_check.extracted_value: Secondary reference-interval study (n=540): first-trimester TSH 5th-95th centile 0.04-3.77 uIU/mL with mean 1.51. Stored T1 central 0.6 sits low within this interval; the stored range 0.1-2.5 is well bracketed by the reported interval.

name: Maternal TSH at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Glinoer 1997: TSH recovers to within non-pregnant range by term.

source_check.source_citation: Hussein et al. 2017, Int J Gen Med (PMC5456279), trimester-specific thyroid reference intervals, Basrah
source_check.quote: Trimester specific normal ranges of TSH in microIU/mL were 0.04-3.77, 0.30-3.21 and 0.6-4.5 microIU/mL, respectively, for first, second and third trimesters.
source_check.extracted_value: Third-trimester TSH 5th-95th centile 0.6-4.5 uIU/mL with mean 1.87. Stored term central 2.0 and range 0.4-4.0 fall within this reported third-trimester interval.

name: Maternal term plasma prolactin
tier_rationale: Tier B from Tyson 1972: prolactin rises ~10x by term to prime lactogenesis; estradiol-driven lactotroph hyperplasia.

source_check.source_citation: Xu et al. 2017, reference intervals for serum prolactin across pregnancy (PMID 29194037)
source_check.quote: first trimester, 621.20-3584.00 microIU/mL; second trimester, 1432.00-5349.68 microIU/mL; third trimester, 4087.33-9733.65 microIU/mL
source_check.extracted_value: Third-trimester prolactin reference interval 4087.33-9733.65 uIU/mL. Using the WHO 3rd IS conversion (1 ng/mL ~ 21.2 uIU/mL, supported by the study's non-pregnant 178.89-757.52 uIU/mL = ~8.4-35.7 ng/mL matching standard prolactin ranges), this is ~193-459 ng/mL. Stored central 200 sits at the low end and the stored range 150-300 overlaps the lower part of the reported interval.

name: Maternal term plasma aldosterone
tier_rationale: Tier B from Wilson 1980: aldosterone rises ~5-10x by term, matching the cumulative sodium retention.

source_check.source_citation: RAAS in normotensive vs hypertensive pregnancy (PMC10454898), Table 2, normotensive group
source_check.quote: Aldosterone, pmol/L First trimester 386.1 (226.2-626.2) Third trimester 970.5 (468.3-1586.0)
source_check.extracted_value: Normotensive third-trimester aldosterone median 970.5 pmol/L (IQR 468.3-1586.0). Converting (1 ng/dL = 27.74 pmol/L): median 35.0 ng/dL, IQR 16.9-57.2 ng/dL. Stored central 40 ng/dL and range 25-60 fall within / overlap the converted interval.

name: Maternal non-pregnant HOMA-IR
tier_rationale: Tier B from Catalano 1991: pre-pregnancy HOMA-IR ~2.0 in non-obese women; baseline against which the ~50% rise is measured.

name: Maternal term HOMA-IR
tier_rationale: Tier B from Catalano 1991: insulin sensitivity falls ~50% by late T3; HOMA-IR roughly doubles. Placental hormones (hPL, progesterone, placental GH) drive insulin resistance.

name: Cortisol-binding globulin at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Carr et al. 1981 J Clin Endocrinol Metab: CBG roughly doubles across normal pregnancy under oestrogen-driven hepatic synthesis (baseline ~35 mg/L → term ~70 mg/L). This is the central driver of the rise in total cortisol; free-cortisol changes are smaller. Replicated across the cortisol-in-pregnancy literature.
notes: Pairs with `cortisol_term_ug_per_dl` — most of the total-cortisol rise is bound to CBG, not biologically free.

name: Plasma renin activity at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Wilson et al. 1980 and corroborated by Cheung & Lafayette 2013: PRA rises ~4–7× pre-pregnancy values (baseline ~1.5 ng/mL/h, term ~10–15 ng/mL/h) as part of the activated renin–angiotensin–aldosterone axis. Despite high circulating renin and aldosterone, pregnant women are remarkably resistant to angiotensin-II pressor effects.
notes: Lognormal distribution reflects the wide observed spread; loss of pressor response in normal pregnancy is itself diagnostic for pre-eclampsia risk.
